factors.summary: Construction and infrastructure spending is expected to drive demand, particularly in the power generation and water management sectors.

evidence.text: Global efforts to limit the emissions of carbon dioxide may have some adverse effect on thermal power investment plans depending on the potential requirements imposed and the timing of compliance by country. [...] The proportion of people living in regions that find it difficult to meet water requirements is expected to double by 2025. We believe that the persistent demand for fresh water during all economic cycles supports continued investments, especially in North America and developing regions.

sentiment.label: positive

factors.summary: Equipment replacement cycles are accelerating due to the need for aftermarket services and upgrades to existing infrastructure.

evidence.text: The worldwide installed base of our products is an important source of aftermarket revenue, where products are intended to maximize operating time of many key industrial processes. We continue to invest in our aftermarket strategy to provide local support to drive customer investments in our offerings and use of our services to replace or repair installed products.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Accelerating equipment replacement cycles suggest a positive sentiment due to increased aftermarket opportunities.

factors.summary: The company is facing some margin compression due to lower sales volumes and supply chain issues, indicating limited pricing power.

evidence.text: Gross profit margin in 2021 of 30.8% increased from 30.3% in 2020. The increase in gross profit margin was primarily attributable to decreased charges and increased savings under our realignment actions as compared to the same period in 2020, a $9.2 million charge of underutilized capacity manufacturing costs expensed related to the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 that did not recur and a mix shift to higher margin aftermarket sales, partially offset by revenue recognized on lower margin original equipment orders and lower customer sales volumes due to supply chain and logistical impacts related to COVID-19.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: The company faces margin compression due to lower sales volumes and supply chain issues, indicating a negative sentiment.

factors.summary: The company faces significant cybersecurity risks with potential for data breaches and financial losses.

evidence.text: Our information technology networks and related systems and devices and those technology systems under control of third parties with whom we do business are critical to the operation of our business and essential to our ability to successfully perform day-to-day operations. These information technology networks and related systems and devices are susceptible to damage, disruptions or shutdowns due to programming errors, defects or other vulnerabilities, power outages, hardware failures, computer viruses, cyber-attacks, malware attacks, ransomware attacks, theft, misconduct by employees or other insiders, misuse, human errors or other events.
evidence.subsection: A significant data breach or disruption to our information technology infrastructure could materially adversely affect our business operations.

evidence.text: Further, we have not seen and do not expect to see an increase in cancellations from our backlog. We therefore expect to continue to deliver on our backlog during 2022, though with a slightly longer cycle time than originally expected.

Since the onset of the pandemic, many of our suppliers have also experienced varying lengths of production and shipping delays related to the COVID-19 pandemic, some of which continue to exist in highly affected countries. Additionally, the global supply chain and logistics constraints that are currently affecting global markets caused additional headwinds in the second half of the year.

The decrease in gross profit margin was primarily attributable to revenue recognized on lower margin original equipment orders, inflationary cost pressure and lower sales volume due to supply chain and logistical impacts related to COVID-19, partially offset by a mix shift to higher margin aftermarket sales, a $5.8 million charge of underutilized capacity manufacturing costs expensed related to the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 that did not recur and decreased charges and increased savings under our realignment actions as compared to the same period in 2020.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Ongoing disruptions and increased costs are causing delays and margin pressures.

factors.summary: Demand trends are mixed, with overall demand improving due to increased vaccinations and market recovery, but have not yet fully returned to pre-pandemic levels, particularly in project-based spending. Some industries are experiencing growth, while others are seeing declines.

evidence.text: As the world continues to make progress against COVID-19 largely through increased vaccinations, we have seen an inflection in our served end-markets as commodity prices and mobility levels increase.

Despite the modest improvement in customer spending, during the fourth quarter of the year we continued to experience customer-driven delays in the witnessing and inspection necessary to take delivery of equipment, which we expect will continue as long as we and our customers continue to experience the supply chain and logistics headwinds described below under the heading 'Supply Chain Impact.'

Bookings in 2021 increased by $317.3 million, or 13.5%, as compared with 2020. The increase included currency benefits of approximately $44 million. The increase in customer bookings was driven by increased orders in the oil and gas, chemical, general and water industries, partially offset by decreased bookings in the power generation industry.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Mixed demand with some recovery but not back to pre-pandemic levels.

factors.summary: The revenue mix is shifting towards higher-margin aftermarket services, which is a positive development. This shift is supported by evidence from both the Bookings and Backlog section and the Business Segments section, indicating a strategic focus on more profitable areas.

evidence.text: Backlog related to aftermarket orders was approximately 38% and 36% of the backlog at December 31, 2021 and 2020, respectively. We expect to recognize revenue on approximately 90% of December 31, 2021 backlog during 2022.

The increase in gross profit margin was primarily attributable to decreased charges and increased savings under our realignment actions as compared to the same period in 2020, a $9.2 million charge of underutilized capacity manufacturing costs expensed related to the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020 that did not recur and a mix shift to higher margin aftermarket sales, partially offset by revenue recognized on lower margin original equipment orders and lower customer sales volumes due to supply chain and logistical impacts related to COVID-19.

sentiment.label: positive
sentiment.rationale: Shift towards higher-margin aftermarket services is a positive development.

factors.summary: The company is experiencing a growing backlog of orders, with new orders outpacing revenue. However, this backlog is at risk due to economic conditions, customer delays, supply chain disruptions, and logistics constraints, which are causing delays in manufacturing and shipping products to customers. These challenges have also led to increased costs and, in some cases, impacted the timely delivery of products, contributing to the backlog increase.

evidence.text: Our backlog represents the value of uncompleted customer orders. While we cannot be certain that reported backlog will be indicative of future results...
Backlog of $2.0 billion at December 31, 2021 increased by $148.7 million, or 8.0%, as compared with December 31, 2020.

These conditions have had an adverse effect on the speed at which we can manufacture and ship our products to customers, and have also led to an increase in logistics, transportation and freight costs, requiring that we diversify our supply chain and, in some instances, source materials from new suppliers. Additionally, these conditions have in some cases impacted our ability to deliver products to customers on time, which has in turn led to an increase in backlog at some of our manufacturing sites.
Backlog of $1.4 billion at December 31, 2021 increased by $132.0 million, or 10.7%, as compared with December 31, 2020.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Growth in backlog is hindered by economic, supply chain, and logistical challenges.

factors.summary: Organic revenue growth is decelerating compared to previous years. While there are signs of improvement, capital expenditures remain below pre-pandemic levels, impacting the overall growth trajectory.

evidence.text: Sales in 2021 decreased by $187.0 million, or 5.0%, as compared with 2020. The decrease included currency benefits of approximately $66 million.

While many of the repair and maintenance projects that were paused by our customers last year as a result of the pandemic have gone forward in 2021 and others will ultimately need to be completed, the timing will largely depend on the duration of the COVID-19 pandemic and how the virus continues to spread in our customers’ various geographies, given the impact of the pandemic on demand, utilization and required maintenance. While we saw some recovery in capital expenditure budgets in 2021 and, therefore, our bookings during the year, capital spending did not approach pre-pandemic levels in 2021. We expect planned capital spending to increase in 2022 but remain below pre-pandemic levels.

Bookings in 2021 increased by $317.3 million, or 13.5%, as compared with 2020. The increase included currency benefits of approximately $44 million. The increase in customer bookings was driven by increased orders in the oil and gas, chemical, general and water industries, partially offset by decreased bookings in the power generation industry.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Decelerating growth and capital spending below pre-pandemic levels indicate challenges.

factors.summary: Aftermarket service revenue is growing faster than new equipment sales, driven by a larger installed base and higher margins. This growth is expected to continue, with a significant portion of the backlog set to be recognized in the coming year.

evidence.text: Backlog related to aftermarket orders was approximately 38% and 36% of the backlog at December 31, 2021 and 2020, respectively. We expect to recognize revenue on approximately 90% of December 31, 2021 backlog during 2022.

The aftermarket portion of our business also helps provide business stability during various economic periods. [...] It is generally a higher margin business compared to our original equipment business and a key component of our profitable growth strategy.

sentiment.label: very_positive
sentiment.rationale: Aftermarket service revenue is growing faster than new equipment sales with higher margins and significant backlog expected to be recognized soon.

factors.summary: The end-market momentum is mixed across different industries. While the oil and gas, chemical, general, and water industries are showing growth or stability, the power generation industry is experiencing a slowdown.

evidence.text: The increase in customer bookings was driven by both original equipment and aftermarket bookings. The increase in sales was driven by increased sales into Asia Pacific and Latin America, partially offset by decreased sales into North America, the Middle East, Africa and Europe.

The oil and gas industry, which represented approximately 35% of our bookings in 2021, experienced a material decrease in capital spending in 2020 but saw repair and maintenance budgets improve in 2021. The chemical industry, representing approximately 24% of our bookings, also saw improved repair and maintenance activity. The power generation and water management industries are showing slower growth but remain stable.
The increase in customer bookings was driven by increased orders in the oil and gas, chemical, general and water industries, partially offset by decreased bookings in the power generation industry.

sentiment.label: neutral
sentiment.rationale: Mixed momentum across industries with some growth and others experiencing slowdowns.

factors.summary: The company is experiencing mixed volume trends across different segments. While unit volumes are declining in certain regions due to supply chain and logistical challenges, there is evidence of growth in the aftermarket segment driven by increased repair and maintenance activity. Additionally, overall unit volumes are declining, with revenue growth being more dependent on price increases rather than real demand.

evidence.text: Sales in 2021 decreased by $187.0 million, or 5.0%, as compared with 2020. The decrease included currency benefits of approximately $66 million.

The overall increase in bookings of 10.6% during 2021 as compared to the same period in 2020 reflects the recovery in demand for our products and services, particularly in the aftermarket segment, which typically has higher margins and more stable demand compared to original equipment sales.

Sales in 2021 decreased $204.9 million, or 7.7%, as compared with 2020. The decrease included currency benefits of approximately $44 million. The decrease was driven primarily by customer original equipment, resulting from decreased customer sales of $86.3 million into North America, $29.0 million into the Middle East, $56.4 million into Asia Pacific, $20.1 million into Africa and $39.0 million into Europe, partially offset by increased sales of $19.3 million into Latin America.

sentiment.label: negative
sentiment.rationale: Declining unit volumes across regions and reliance on price increases rather than real demand.
